item.title: My 2019 last.fm
item.description:
last.fm is a social network in which users can keep track of their own musical activity through the so called scrobbles (the log of your listened song). This service was created in 2002, when it was necessary to install a software in order to scrobble. Recently, however, the plataform was integrated to music streaming services such as Spotify, Deezer, Tidal and Google Play Music. Thus, it has become much easier to keep track of your own musical activity. last.fm shows weekly (in the free version), monthly and annual (in the premium version) statistics, concerning the number of listened songs, artists, albums, genres, temporal patterns and comparison with other last.fm users. In addition, the plataform allows users to find people with similar musical tastes.

Here, I perform a statistical analysis of my last.fm profile in 2019 (and who knows turn this an annual thing). I start by using this tool to export my profile’s listening history. Then, I use Python language to parse data and make some visualizations. My code is available at: https://github.com/ansesu/lastfm/. So, here we go! First of all, some numbers that summarize my 2019.

Table of Contents

  • Overview
  • My 2019 most listened songs
  • My 2019 most listened artists
  • My 2019 most listened albums
  • My 2019 most listened new artists
  • Visualizations
  • References

Overview

  • Total plays: 10,215

  • Total songs: 2,270

  • Total artists: 555

  • Total albums: 1,027

My 2019 most listened songs

  1. Pick U Up - Foster the People (159 plays)
  2. Imagination - Foster the People (102 plays)
  3. Style - Foster the People (65 plays)
  4. Half of the Way - Vulfpeck (61 plays)
  5. Wait For The Moment - Vulfpeck (57 plays)
  6. Worst Nites - Foster the People (52 plays)
  7. 1612 - Vulfpeck (51 plays)
  8. Don’t Stop (Color on the Walls) - Foster the People (49 plays)
  9. Goats in Trees - Foster the People (49 plays)
  10. Helena Beat - Foster the People (48 plays)

My 2019 most listened artists

  1. Vulfpeck (1785 plays)
  2. Foster the People (1711 plays)
  3. Saint Motel (427 plays)
  4. The Kooks (377 plays)
  5. Khruangbin (264 plays)
  6. Red Hot Chili Peppers (247 plays)
  7. The Strokes (201 plays)
  8. Jamiroquai (185 plays)
  9. J Rabbit (180 plays)
  10. Wednesday Campanella (162 plays)

My 2019 most listened albums

  1. Torches - Foster the People (418 plays)
  2. Supermodel - Foster the People (339 plays)
  3. Thrill of the Arts - Vulfpeck (312 plays)
  4. Sacred Hearts Club - Foster the People (302 plays)
  5. hill climber - Vulfpeck (286 plays)
  6. The Beautiful Game - Vulfpeck (280 plays)
  7. Saintmotelevision - Saint Motel (236 plays)
  8. Pick U Up - Foster the People (216 plays)
  9. Fugue State - Vulfpeck (213 plays)
  10. Mr Finish Line - Vulfpeck (194 plays)

My 2019 most listened new artists

  1. Theo Katzman (66 plays)
  2. Beach Fossils (64 plays)
  3. Ripe (40 plays)
  4. DJ Bean Ornish (28 plays)
  5. Breakestra (23 plays)
  6. Michael Haydn (22 plays)
  7. おかん (19 plays)
  8. Madeon (18 plays)
  9. Scary Goldings (17 plays)
  10. Ozawa Kenji (17 plays)
  11. 小沢健二 (14 plays)
  12. Aqueous (12 plays)

Visualizations

Now, I present some visualizations with some interesting statistics (at least for me). The figure below shows the number of songs listened in each period of the day in 2019. Usually, when I am programming, studying or working out, I listen to music. In this sense, the higher musical activity may be a proxy of my productivity (and the low activity shows that I normally don’t wake up early).

Figure: Number of songs listened in each period of the day in 2019.

The figure below shows the number of songs listened in each month of 2019. In July and August, I traveled for some extracurricular activity and, thus, didn’t have much time to listen to music.

Figure: number of songs listened in each month of 2019.

The figure below shows the number of songs listened in each day of the week in 2019. As mentioned before, I usually listen to music when I am performing some activity, so the count is naturally higher in weekdays. Specially, I believe my productivity is higher on Mondays.

Figure: number of songs listened in each day of the week in 2019.

I also analyzed the listening streaks for artists and songs. I tend to listen to music in shuffle mode, so, a long streak indicates that I am actually addicted to that specific artist or song. The figure below shows the listening streaks for artists in 2019. At the end of 2018, I found a amazing band called Vulfpeck (which I strongly recommend) and ended up listening to it a lot in 2019. This was my biggest 2019 artist streak. Other honorable mentions: Foster the People (all the way!) and Beach Fossils, this last one presented by my lab colleague Gustavo.

Figure: Streaks listening the same artist in 2019.

The figure below shows my song streaks in 2019. In summary, I usually don't listen to songs on repeat.

Figure: Streaks listening the same track in 2019.

Next, I applied the concept of Shannon entropy to the distribution of artists listened. The normalized Shanon entropy is described by the equation [1] $$ H = \text{Diversity} = -\dfrac{1}{\log n}\sum_i P_i \log P_i $$ where $n$ is the total number of artists and $P_i$ is the fraction of songs from the $i$th artist. The entropy indicates the quantity of information contained in the probability distribution of artists. Values close to one ($\approx 1$) reveal that information is equally distributed among artists or, in other words, I listen to them in the same proportion. Conversely, values close to zero ($\approx 0$) indicate that one of the artists dominates my musical sessions. Therefore, I consider that the entropy is a kind of musical diversity (or heterogeinity) considering the artists I listen, which is different than being eclectic. The figure below shows the entropy for each week in 2019. The entropy exhibit smaller values exactly when I had my longest streaks. Also, it seems that, during a week, I tend to listen to a set of artists homogeneously.

Figure: Diversity of artist (as measured by Shannon entropy) in each week of 2019.

The figure below shows the entropy for each day of the week in 2019. Weekdays present smaller values of entropy, what may suggest that I tend to listen to a specific kind of music (and consequently of artist) when I am performing my activities.

Figure: Diversity of artist (as measured by Shannon entropy) in each day of the week in 2019.

Browsing the internet, I found a cool visualization called stream wave chart. This kind of figure shows the temporal evolution of the volume of songs listened (given by a filled area) of a set of specific artists. I found it really awesome and tried to make my own stream chart. The above figures were all made using the Python package Bokeh. But it turns out this kind of visualization is not available on Bokeh. I searched for some alternatives and found out the Altair package (also from Python). Then, I selected all the artists with at least 30 reproductions in 2019, which yielded a set of 21 unique artists. The figure below shows the evolution of my reproduction volume in 2019. You can click on the names to select a specific artist. Also, you can do your own last.fm wave chart in a non-pythonic way by using this site (try it out!).

Figure: Temporal evolution of the volume of reproductions of top artists in 2019.

Finally, I used flourish.io app to make a bar chart race (which has been really popular in social media recently). This is an alternative to visualize the temporal evolution of the listening history, but, this time, considering the cumulative number of reproductions.

References

[1] Shannon, Claude E., A Mathematical Theory of Communication. Bell System Technical Journal. 27 (3): 379–423 (1948)

item.title: PEP8 - Best practices in styling for Python programming
item.description:
Recently, I had to go back to some code from my master’s degree. I realized the code was bad written and hard to read. In order to solve this problem, I decided to study what are considered the best styling practices when coding in Python. This post is therefore kind of a cheatsheet for future reference. I intend to keep updating this page as I progress my research in that matter. I used the PEP 8 from the official website python.org as resource. The PEPs, Python Enhancement Proposals, are documents created with the purpose of instructing Python users regarding standards, new features, processes or environments [1]. Specifically, PEP 8 points out the style standards for Python users, in order the code to be easily readable [2]. So, here we go!

(The code was copied and pasted from [2] although the commments and explanation may be different)

Table of Contents

  • Code layout
    • Indentation
    • Space or tab?
    • Maximum line length
  • References

Code layout

Indentation

Standard: 4 spaces per indentation level

Some possible ways to write:

# Opening delimiter alignment "(".
foo = long_function_name(var_one, var_two,
                         var_three, var_four)

# Addition of 4 spaces (an extra level of indentation) to distinguish arguments from the rest.
def long_function_name(
        var_one, var_two, var_three,
        var_four):
    print(var_one)

# Hanging indents should add a level.
foo = long_function_name(
    var_one, var_two,
    var_three, var_four)

However, in some cases, you may choose which option is more adequate to the situation:

# Wrong. Further indentation required as indentation is not distinguishable.
def long_function_name(
    var_one, var_two, var_three,
    var_four):
    print(var_one)
# Correct.
def long_function_name(var_one, var_two, 
                        var_three, var_four):
    print(var_one)
Indentation: if-statement

When the conditional part of an if-statement is too long, there may be a problem when breaking it into multiple lines:

if (this_is_one_thing and
    that_is_another_thing):
    do_something()

It is not possible to distinguish the statement and its content exactly because if ( contains four characters. Two possible options are:

# Add a comment between lines.
if (this_is_one_thing and
    that_is_another_thing):
    # Since both conditions are true, we can frobnicate.
    do_something()

# Add some extra indentation on the conditional continuation line.
if (this_is_one_thing
        and that_is_another_thing):
    do_something()
Indentation: brace/bracket/parenthesis

The brace/bracket/parenthesis on multiline constructs should line up with the first non-whitespace character of the last line of the list:

my_list = [
    1, 2, 3,
    4, 5, 6,
    ]
result = some_function_that_takes_arguments(
    'a', 'b', 'c',
    'd', 'e', 'f',
    )

or align with the first character of the line that starts the multiline construct:

my_list = [
    1, 2, 3,
    4, 5, 6,
]
result = some_function_that_takes_arguments(
    'a', 'b', 'c',
    'd', 'e', 'f',
)

Space or tab?

Standard: spaces are preferred over tabs.

If a code’s indentation default method is tab, you can use tab to match the current style. However, when starting a project, spaces (four) all the way!

Maximum line length

Standard: 79 characters maximum line width. For docstrings and comments, the maximum width should be up to 72 characters.

I thought it was a little strange to limit line width up to 79 characters, but PEP 8 gives two good reasons for choosing this threshold (besides standardization):

  • This length may allow opening two codes side by side, which, in turn, is useful when reviewing them for example;
  • Some text editors have default wrapping of up to 80 characters horizontally. Sizes bigger than that may disrupt the code visual structure, making it harder to read. Therefore, the maximum line width of 79 characters is a safe limit (by one character).

However, for teams that prefer longer lines, and are working in a code exclusively/primarly maintained by them, if a consensus is established, there is no problem in increasing the line width up to 99 characters.

To help standardization, Jupyter Noteboook Extensions offer an tool called ruler that displays a vertical line indicating the character threshold as specified by the user. The figure below shows a piece of my code and the ruler tool enabled.

In order to break lines, it is recommended to encapsulate the expressions inside parenthesis, braces or brackets. When this option is not available, it is possible to use backslashes to break lines (although this is the least preferable option). For instance, when declaring a with or assert statement, implicit continuation is not possible, then we can use backslashes

with open('/path/to/some/file/you/want/to/read') as file_1, \
     open('/path/to/some/file/being/written', 'w') as file_2:
    file_2.write(file_1.read())

References

[1] PEP 1 – PEP Purpose and Guidelines.

[2] PEP 8 – Style Guide for Python Code.

item.title: City size and the spreading of COVID-19 in Brazil
First post
item.description:
This website was created in order to keep track of my academic progress, and with the intention of posting about matters that I may find interesting. The perspectives here shown are not necessarily associated with the ComplexLab.

Fun fact: this website, ansesu, is name after the initials of André Seiji Sunahara, since its abbreviation may sound a little rude.

The photo shown below pictures me and my ComplexLab friends at the time of my dissertation defense.
